proposed_from.topic: Partial identification of dynamic treatment effects under non-Markov assignments and imperfect compliance
proposed_from.novelty_justification: {"repo_axis":"The closest in-catalogue proposal is pid_late_bounded_defiers: Sharp complier-LATE bounds from a collapsed Balke--Pearl principal-stratum envelope, which is static and one-period. The adjacent burned flagship_explore/f1 proposal targeted sustained-regime full-history separation; the present proposal instead targets a two-stage pure-never-taker mixture-subtraction face for theta_C0^(2).","published_axis":"BalkePearl1997 gives static binary-IV bounds; BugniGaoObradovicVelez2024 handles covariate-adaptive imperfect-compliance ATE/ATT sets; DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2024 gives automated polynomial-programming bounds; ChenZhang2023 studies Bellman-style IV-optimal dynamic regimes. None states the fixed-target assignment-memory strict-gain diagnostic created by separating the pure never-taker cell from the mixed untreated branch."}
seed_list: Prior work SojitraSyrgkanis2024 establishes point identification of some dynamic LATEs under one-sided noncompliance and extra cross-period conditions. Our seed conjectures that when those conditions fail, non-Markov second-stage assignment still strictly tightens sustained-effect bounds because past encouragement splits first-period principal-stratum mixtures.

Prior work BugniGaoObradovicVelez2024 characterizes static imperfect-compliance bounds under covariate-adaptive randomization. Our seed conjectures that dynamic post-baseline assignment probabilities have additional identifying content because the previous instrument becomes a compliance-revealing state variable.

Prior work Han2023 maps dynamic-regime welfare comparisons to LP-based partial orderings. Our seed conjectures that a single sustained treatment contrast has a sharper instrument-memory interval than the welfare-ordering LP would reveal after Markov coarsening.

Prior work MichaelCuiLorchTchetgen2024 identifies time-varying-IV MSMM parameters under compliance-type restrictions. Our seed conjectures that dropping the no-unobserved-compliance-predictor condition does not erase all information because non-Markov randomization leaves observable mixture-splitting constraints.

Prior work ArtmanBhattacharyaErtefaieLynchMcKayJohnson2024 models partial compliance in SMARTs through principal-strata MSMs. Our seed conjectures that in a nonparametric SMART, assignment memory can make principal-stratum regime means partially identifiable without a Bayesian compliance model.

Prior work BalkePearl1997 establishes sharp static IV bounds by latent response types. Our seed conjectures that in two periods the same response-type polytope develops a new strict-containment face when Z1 is retained in the Z2 assignment rule.

Prior work DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2024 would numerically bound the finite discrete problem. Our seed conjectures that the strict gain from instrument memory has a closed-form design diagnostic tied to mixture splitting, not just a solver certificate.

Prior work SwansonHernanMillerRobinsRichardson2018 reviews static binary-IV partial identification. Our seed conjectures that dynamic sustained effects can be bounded more tightly than repeated static IV bounds because the first instrument changes later assignment probabilities.

Prior work ManskiPepper2000 sharpens bounds with monotone instrumental-variable or response assumptions. Our seed conjectures that assignment-memory sharpening is orthogonal to shape restrictions and can occur with unrestricted second-period compliance.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work SojitraSyrgkanis2024 establishes point identification of some dynamic LATEs under one-sided noncompliance and extra cross-period conditions. Our seed conjectures that when those conditions fail, non-Markov second-stage assignment still strictly tightens sustained-effect bounds because past encouragement splits first-period principal-stratum mixtures.

anchor_paper.author: Sojitra and Syrgkanis
anchor_paper.claim: Identifies dynamic LATEs for binary encouragement/treatment settings under one-sided noncompliance, with multi-period effects requiring additional cross-period conditions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extends point ID to partial ID, but not blocked: the phenomenon is strict principal-stratum deconvolution from instrument memory, not a mere weakening of assumptions.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from SojitraSyrgkanis2024 because their history-adaptive DTR setup already conditions on past encouragements.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their results target point identification under additional assumptions; they do not characterize sharp intervals or strict tightening from retaining Z1 in e2 after those assumptions fail.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work BugniGaoObradovicVelez2024 characterizes static imperfect-compliance bounds under covariate-adaptive randomization. Our seed conjectures that dynamic post-baseline assignment probabilities have additional identifying content because the previous instrument becomes a compliance-revealing state variable.

anchor_paper.author: Bugni, Gao, Obradovic, and Velez
anchor_paper.claim: Characterizes ATE and ATT identified sets under covariate-adaptive randomization and imperfect compliance.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extends static to dynamic, but not blocked: the new term is post-baseline instrument memory that splits latent compliance mixtures.
seed_details.referee_objection: This follows from their CAR formulas with history treated as a covariate.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their assignment strata are baseline design strata; here Z1 is post-baseline and statistically entangled with latent compliance after conditioning on D1.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Han2023 maps dynamic-regime welfare comparisons to LP-based partial orderings. Our seed conjectures that a single sustained treatment contrast has a sharper instrument-memory interval than the welfare-ordering LP would reveal after Markov coarsening.

anchor_paper.author: Han
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Uses linear programs to construct sharp partial welfare orderings over dynamic treatment regimes with IVs.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Target variation from welfare ordering to sustained contrast, but not blocked: the proposed phenomenon is strict loss from Markov coarsening of assignment memory.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is just a special case of Han2023's LP framework.
seed_details.rebuttal: Han's framework supplies LP machinery but not the analytic strict-containment theorem comparing full instrument memory with Markovized assignment.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work MichaelCuiLorchTchetgen2024 identifies time-varying-IV MSMM parameters under compliance-type restrictions. Our seed conjectures that dropping the no-unobserved-compliance-predictor condition does not erase all information because non-Markov randomization leaves observable mixture-splitting constraints.

anchor_paper.author: Michael, Cui, Lorch, and Tchetgen Tchetgen
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Identifies and estimates marginal structural mean model parameters with time-varying IVs under conditions on compliance types.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Weakens an assumption, but not blocked: the claim is a residual sharp-bound mechanism from observed assignment memory.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate by taking their model nonparametrically and replacing equality with bounds.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their estimand and restrictions are MSMM-specific; they do not analyze finite response-type sharp sets or memory-vs-Markov containment.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work ArtmanBhattacharyaErtefaieLynchMcKayJohnson2024 models partial compliance in SMARTs through principal-strata MSMs. Our seed conjectures that in a nonparametric SMART, assignment memory can make principal-stratum regime means partially identifiable without a Bayesian compliance model.

anchor_paper.author: Artman, Bhattacharya, Ertefaie, Lynch, McKay, and Johnson
anchor_paper.claim: Defines compliance-stratum dynamic-regime means in SMARTs and estimates them via a marginal structural/principal-stratification model.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: n/a — orthogonal to blocklist; the seed asks for nonparametric sharp bounds behind a modeled SMART estimand.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is an immediate nonparametric version of their principal-strata model.
seed_details.rebuttal: Their Bayesian MSM does not provide sharp finite-support identified sets or a design-memory strictness criterion.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work BalkePearl1997 establishes sharp static IV bounds by latent response types. Our seed conjectures that in two periods the same response-type polytope develops a new strict-containment face when Z1 is retained in the Z2 assignment rule.

anchor_paper.author: Balke and Pearl
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Derives tight nonparametric bounds for static treatment effects in studies with imperfect compliance.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extends T=1 to T=2, but not blocked: the new object is a strict-containment face caused by non-Markov assignment memory.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is a direct finite-dimensional Balke-Pearl LP with more variables.
seed_details.rebuttal: The generic LP is immediate, but the proposed strictness/minimal-boundary theorem is not present in the static result.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2024 would numerically bound the finite discrete problem. Our seed conjectures that the strict gain from instrument memory has a closed-form design diagnostic tied to mixture splitting, not just a solver certificate.

anchor_paper.author: Duarte, Finkelstein, Knox, Mummolo, and Shpitser
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Shows discrete causal queries can be bounded automatically by optimization over admissible data-generating processes.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: n/a — orthogonal to blocklist; the seed seeks an analytic diagnostic inside a general computational class.
seed_details.referee_objection: Autobounds already solves this problem numerically.
seed_details.rebuttal: A numerical solver does not state when or why non-Markov assignment memory strictly tightens the interval.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work SwansonHernanMillerRobinsRichardson2018 reviews static binary-IV partial identification. Our seed conjectures that dynamic sustained effects can be bounded more tightly than repeated static IV bounds because the first instrument changes later assignment probabilities.

anchor_paper.author: Swanson, Hernan, Miller, Robins, and Richardson
anchor_paper.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association
anchor_paper.claim: Reviews partial identification of ATEs with binary instruments, treatments, and outcomes.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extends static to dynamic, but not blocked: the proposed mechanism is cross-period assignment-memory coupling, not repeated static bounding.
seed_details.referee_objection: Repeated application of static IV bounds should cover this.
seed_details.rebuttal: Repeated static bounds drop the Z1-dependent Z2 assignment probabilities that split first-period latent strata.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work ManskiPepper2000 sharpens bounds with monotone instrumental-variable or response assumptions. Our seed conjectures that assignment-memory sharpening is orthogonal to shape restrictions and can occur with unrestricted second-period compliance.

anchor_paper.author: Manski and Pepper
anchor_paper.claim: Introduces monotone instrumental-variable restrictions and shows how monotone assumptions sharpen treatment-response bounds.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: n/a — orthogonal to blocklist; sharpening comes from randomized design memory, not another monotonicity condition.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is just another monotonicity/shaping restriction in disguise.
seed_details.rebuttal: The second-period compliance map is unrestricted; the tightening comes from observed assignment probabilities and latent mixture splitting.

proposed_from.literature_map: BalkePearl1997 (JASA, https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1997.10474074) gives sharp static binary-IV bounds under imperfect compliance; open gap is dynamic assignment memory. Manski1990 (AER) supplies bounded-outcome partial-ID logic but not dynamic IV compliance. ManskiPepper2000 (Econometrica) sharpens bounds via monotone restrictions; tension is whether design-memory rather than shape restrictions can sharpen. SwansonHernanMillerRobinsRichardson2018 (JASA) reviews binary-IV partial identification and leaves sustained dynamic-regime effects outside static formulas. MichaelCuiLorchTchetgen2024 (JASA, https://ideas.repec.org/a/taf/jnlasa/v119y2024i546p1240-1251.html) identifies MSMM parameters with time-varying IVs under compliance-type restrictions; tension is point-ID modeling versus nonparametric bounds. SojitraSyrgkanis2024 (arXiv, https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.01463) identifies dynamic LATEs under one-sided noncompliance plus cross-period conditions; open gap is partial ID when those conditions fail. Han2023 (JASA, https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2023.2238941) uses LPs for partial welfare ordering of dynamic regimes; gap is one named sustained contrast and assignment-memory strictness. ArtmanBhattacharyaErtefaieLynchMcKayJohnson2024 (Annals of Applied Statistics, https://doi.org/10.1214/21-AOAS1586) models partial compliance in SMARTs, showing applied demand but not sharp nonparametric bounds. BugniGaoObradovicVelez2024 (arXiv, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.08419) characterizes static CAR bounds under imperfect compliance; phenomenon suggests assignment probabilities can matter. DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2024 (JASA, https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2023.2216909) automates discrete sharp bounds; open gap is analytic criteria explaining when a design feature strictly tightens a bound.
